content.value: RAM stores data that can be both read and written, with any address accessible in roughly equal time ('random' access, as opposed to sequential). It is volatile - contents are lost when power is removed.

A memory array is organized as a grid of address lines decoded into word-select lines, much like the decoders studied earlier. An n-bit address can select one of 2^n unique memory words, and a separate set of lines determines how many bits wide each word is.

ROM stores fixed data that is normally only read, not written, during normal operation. Internally a ROM is an AND array (fixed, generating all 2^n minterms from n address lines) feeding a programmable OR array that selects which minterms combine to form each output bit.

A PLA has both a programmable AND array and a programmable OR array. Unlike a ROM, it does not need to generate every possible minterm - only the product terms actually used by your design - making it more efficient for functions that don't need all 2^n minterms.

A PAL has a programmable AND array but a fixed OR array. It's cheaper and simpler to manufacture than a PLA, at the cost of some flexibility, and was historically popular for implementing custom combinational logic before modern FPGAs became standard.

items: ROM: fixed AND array, programmable OR array
PLA: programmable AND array, programmable OR array
PAL: programmable AND array, fixed OR array

content.value: A quick way to remember the difference: ROM is the least flexible (good for table lookups), PLA is the most flexible (but most expensive), and PAL sits in between.

quiz.question: Which memory type is volatile (loses data without power)?
What distinguishes a PLA from a ROM?
options: A PLA's AND array is also programmable, so it doesn't need every minterm
A PLA has no OR array at all
A PLA cannot implement combinational logic

quiz.question: A PAL has:
options: A fixed AND array and programmable OR array
A programmable AND array and a fixed OR array
